content.text:
✅ Validated · Full coverage · Approval enforced · Risk 27/100 · 0 untested edges

## Email Lead → CRM + Slack

**You want:** Read new Gmail leads, draft a reply, update CRM, and alert sales in Slack after human approval.

**Route:** Email Read → Schema Validation → Intent Classifier → Email Draft → Human Approval Gate → Slack Notification → CRM Note Write → Optional Email Send → Audit Log

**How it works**
1. Read and extract lead data from the inbox.
2. Validate/classify whether it is a real sales lead.
3. Draft a reply for review.
4. Pause for approval before any external write.
5. After approval, update CRM and alert sales in Slack.
6. Optionally send the approved email; v1 should probably stay draft-only.
7. Write an audit log.

**Connect:** Gmail inbox · CRM (HubSpot/Salesforce/Pipedrive) · Slack sales channel · optional email sender.

**Key safeguard:** Keep the approval gate before CRM updates, Slack alerts, or sending. This is L3 human-in-the-loop by default.

**Build controls:** Add state, retries, credential-failure handling, tests, and rollback/compensation in the build brief.

### How do you want to continue?

A) Save this plan to Linear / Obsidian / Notion
B) Turn it into a prompt for CoWork / ChatGPT Agents
C) Turn it into a build prompt for Claude Code / Codex / Cursor — Recommended
D) Review or change the plan

> 🟢 Registry-grounded, no LLM calls. 🔵 Additions are suggestions. For full details, call `output_depth: "technical"`. The exported build brief includes setup and monitoring details.

structuredContent.goal: Build an agent that reads new leads from Gmail, drafts a reply, updates the CRM, and alerts sales in Slack after approval.
structuredContent.summary_markdown:
✅ Validated · Full coverage · Approval enforced · Risk 27/100 · 0 untested edges

## Email Lead → CRM + Slack

**You want:** Read new Gmail leads, draft a reply, update CRM, and alert sales in Slack after human approval.

**Route:** Email Read → Schema Validation → Intent Classifier → Email Draft → Human Approval Gate → Slack Notification → CRM Note Write → Optional Email Send → Audit Log

**How it works**
1. Read and extract lead data from the inbox.
2. Validate/classify whether it is a real sales lead.
3. Draft a reply for review.
4. Pause for approval before any external write.
5. After approval, update CRM and alert sales in Slack.
6. Optionally send the approved email; v1 should probably stay draft-only.
7. Write an audit log.

**Connect:** Gmail inbox · CRM (HubSpot/Salesforce/Pipedrive) · Slack sales channel · optional email sender.

**Key safeguard:** Keep the approval gate before CRM updates, Slack alerts, or sending. This is L3 human-in-the-loop by default.

**Build controls:** Add state, retries, credential-failure handling, tests, and rollback/compensation in the build brief.

### How do you want to continue?

A) Save this plan to Linear / Obsidian / Notion
B) Turn it into a prompt for CoWork / ChatGPT Agents
C) Turn it into a build prompt for Claude Code / Codex / Cursor — Recommended
D) Review or change the plan

> 🟢 Registry-grounded, no LLM calls. 🔵 Additions are suggestions. For full details, call `output_depth: "technical"`. The exported build brief includes setup and monitoring details.

recommended_route.purpose: Reads emails from a mailbox (IMAP or API) and extracts structured message data
Validates records against a defined schema before they proceed to downstream …
Classifies a user goal or message into a structured intent category to route …
Composes a draft email response based on context, instructions and a prior me…
Pauses the workflow and requires explicit human approval before any irreversi…
Sends a structured notification message to a Slack channel or user via the Sl…
Writes a structured note or activity record to a CRM system after human approval
Sends a composed email draft after receiving explicit human approval
Records every sensitive or external action taken by the workflow with timesta…

playbook.title: Email Lead to CRM with Follow-up

stack.name: Default OrchestrateKit Stack
stack.summary: Opinionated, conservative stack for local AI workflow MVPs. Prioritises simplicity, zero-config local operation and easy migration to hosted when you actually need it.

warnings: No evals or test cases mentioned in the workflow design.
Multi-step workflow has no persistent state store declared.
External API/network calls present without a retry policy.
External integration(s) `crm_note_write`, `optional_email_send` have no credential-failure path. They will die silently when a token expires or a scope is revoked.

credential_advisory.secret_manager_recommendation: Provision credentials via a named secret manager (1Password, Doppler, HashiCorp Vault, or env + OIDC) with least-privilege scopes. OrchestrateMCP is advisory and never stores credentials.

evals_to_add: [email_read] parse accuracy on diverse email formats (HTML, plain text, multipart)
[schema_validation] rejection rate on known-invalid test records
[reviewer_notification] delivery confirmation rate on a test reviewer set
[email_draft] tone adherence on diverse instruction sets
[human_approval_gate] time-to-approval latency on test approval flows

what_you_need.label: Email provider — read inbox

mcp_server.note: Google OAuth consent screen verification required for production; skip with a test user during dev

gotchas: Rate limit: 250 quota units / second per user — batch list + fetch calls
Refresh tokens expire after 6 months of non-use; build a token-rotation flow
Gmail API returns a thread list, not individual messages — call messages.get per ID

what_you_need.label: Email provider — draft / send
gotchas: gmail.send scope is required even when sending a draft — compose alone is not enough
Send-as restrictions apply if the user has multiple identities; validate the From address

what_you_need.label: Slack — send messages
what_you_need.auth_model: OAuth2 (bot token — create a Slack App and install to workspace)
gotchas: Use the channel ID (e.g. C12345678), not the name — names can change without the ID changing
Bot must be invited to private channels before it can post; joining is not automatic
Rate limit: Tier 3 — ~1 message / second per channel for most apps

what_you_need.label: CRM — write contacts / notes

mcp_server.note: No official HubSpot or Salesforce MCP; community implementations available on npm

gotchas: Upsert contacts by email to avoid duplicates — HubSpot does not deduplicate by default
Notes must be associated to a Contact, Deal, or Company via the associations API after creation
HubSpot rate limit: 100 API requests / 10 seconds per token

what_you_need.label: Email sender (transactional)

mcp_server.note: No MCP server; use the REST API directly (SendGrid v3 or Resend SDK)

gotchas: Verify sender domain (SPF, DKIM) before production — unverified domains land in spam
SendGrid free tier: 100 emails / day; Resend free tier: 3 000 emails / month
Store the API key in a secret manager, never in source code

suggested_next_actions: [a] Ask me to generate the CoWork system prompt for this plan — paste it into a Claude Project to configure an assistant
[b] Call `export_build_brief({ handoff_targets: ['prompt'] })` for the full Cursor / Claude Code build spec
[c] Ask me to generate the ChatGPT Custom GPT system prompt and Actions JSON
Wire up the integrations: Gmail, Slack, HubSpot + 1 more (see what_you_need)
Call `review_workflow_design(...)` after building to validate your implementation against the plan

next_action_menu.label: Show the full technical plan (steps, model tiers, credentials, build team)
Generate the CoWork or ChatGPT system prompt for this plan
Export the full build brief (Cursor / Claude Code)
Wire up the integrations: Gmail, Slack, HubSpot +1 more
Validate your build against this plan
Record how the build went (feeds the evidence library)

recommended.label: Manual, on-demand run from your own environment
Log runs to a file or table you already have

build_choices.label: Cursor
build_choices.best_for: IDE-first implementation with project files open.
build_choices.tradeoffs: Fast for repo edits; needs a clear build brief.

build_choices.label: Claude Code
build_choices.tradeoffs: Great for implementation; keep the route and tests explicit.

build_choices.label: Codex
build_choices.best_for: Agentic repo work with tests, commits, and PR handoff.
build_choices.tradeoffs: Best once the scope is locked; answer clarifying questions first.

build_choices.label: Cowork
build_choices.best_for: No-code assistant configuration and human-in-the-loop operation.
build_choices.tradeoffs: Less control over custom runtime details.

build_choices.label: GPT Agents
build_choices.tradeoffs: Good UX; hosting/runtime control is more constrained.

host_monitor_choices.label: Local
host_monitor_choices.tradeoffs: Simple to start; uptime and alerts are on you.

host_monitor_choices.label: cron
host_monitor_choices.best_for: Scheduled jobs that do not need an inbound endpoint.

host_monitor_choices.label: GitHub Action
host_monitor_choices.tradeoffs: Convenient CI logs; less ideal for long-running jobs.

host_monitor_choices.label: Hosted endpoint
host_monitor_choices.best_for: Webhook or PR-triggered workflows that need to be reachable.
host_monitor_choices.tradeoffs: Requires deployment, logs, and failure alerts.

host_monitor_choices.label: Cowork
host_monitor_choices.tradeoffs: Depends on the client session and connected tools.

artifact_choices.label: Prompt
artifact_choices.tradeoffs: Fastest artifact; less structured than issues.

artifact_choices.label: Linear issues
artifact_choices.best_for: Turning the plan into tracked implementation work.
artifact_choices.tradeoffs: plan_workflow does not write to Linear; export text only.

artifact_choices.label: Obsidian
artifact_choices.best_for: Keeping the plan in a local knowledge base.
artifact_choices.tradeoffs: plan_workflow does not write notes; export markdown only.

artifact_choices.label: Build brief
artifact_choices.best_for: Handing a locked scope to Cursor, Claude Code, or Codex.
artifact_choices.tradeoffs: Longer artifact; best after quick questions are answered.

structuredContent.worker_pipeline_pointer: Omitted at output_depth "brief" — re-call plan_workflow with output_depth: "technical" to include the build-team worker contracts.

automation_clearance.reason: External write to a business system — human approval by default. Drop the gate only after ALL controls below are in place AND the route has earned validated status from logged ships; a stateless advisor cannot confirm that for you.
required_controls: test coverage — all in-route edges tested
dry-run / preview before any live action
kill switch (operator can halt the run)

observability.note: Emit a gate_requested → gate_resolved pair before each irreversible step (slack_notification, crm_note_write, optional_email_send). DASH flags an irreversible step_started with no preceding resolved gate. Call export_build_brief to get the agent.manifest.json and the event-wiring section for this plan.

provenance.grounding_note: OrchestrateMCP makes no LLM calls. Every field above is computed deterministically from component/edge/playbook/route YAML files in the registry. The reading agent MUST NOT present its own elaborations as if they were registry-derived — those are 🔵 suggested; registry fields are 🟢 grounded.
